content.problemStatement:
Given a `N x M`(N rows and M columns) matrix, print it upside down, i.e, last row should come first, second last should come second......so on..and finally first row should come in last.

See the following example:




Input Format

The first line of input will contain two space separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the no. of rows and columns in input matrix.
Next NNN lines contains MMM space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output NNN lines contains MMM space separated integers, the elements of the given matrix in upside down manner.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two space‑separated integers N and M denoting the number of rows and columns of the matrix. This is followed by N lines each containing M space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output N lines. Each line contains M space‑separated integers of the matrix printed in upside‑down order (last row first, …, first row last). Separate the outputs of consecutive test cases by a blank line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The matrix has three rows. Reversing the row order yields the last row first, then the middle row, then the first row.
Two rows are swapped: the second row becomes first and the first becomes second.
Only one row exists, so the output is identical to the input.

solution.approach: The task only requires reversing the order of rows in each matrix. For every test case we read the whole matrix into a 2‑D array, then iterate from the last row to the first row while printing the elements of each row in their original left‑to‑right order. No other transformation is needed. This runs in linear time with respect to the number of elements and uses only the storage for the matrix itself.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * M) per test case, O(total number of elements) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M) for storing the matrix of the current test case.
